chunk_summary: Persistent diarrhea in India is a high-mortality syndrome requiring stool phenotype-based nutrition strategies and early referral for growth and age-related red flags.
learning_objectives: Understand stool phenotypes and their implications for nutritional management in persistent diarrhea.
Identify growth and age-related red flags for early referral in GI disorders.
Differentiate between secondary lactose intolerance, fat malabsorption, and inflammatory disease based on stool characteristics.
Apply India-specific guidelines for triage and first-pass assessment in pediatric GI disorders.
key_facts: Persistent diarrhea is defined by IAP as lasting >14 days with nutritional deterioration, most cases occurring in infancy (60% before 6 months, 90% below 1 year).
Watery diarrhea with perianal excoriation and explosive stools suggests secondary lactose intolerance; prioritize hydration and low-lactose trials.
Bulky, greasy, foul-smelling stools indicate fat malabsorption; avoid indiscriminate fat restriction until etiology is clarified.
Bloody stools, nocturnal symptoms, weight loss, anemia, and low albumin suggest inflammatory disease; expedite definitive evaluation.
Neonatal profuse diarrhea with recurrent dehydration may indicate congenital diarrheal disorders or immunodeficiency-related enteropathy.
reference_quote: Persistent diarrhea in India should be treated as a high-mortality syndrome rather than a prolonged episode of acute gastroenteritis. (IAP Standard Treatment Guidelines: Persistent diarrhea, Chapter 030)
document_key_topics: Differentiation of GI disorders based on stool characteristics
